intro:
The Way (Hebrew derek; Greek hodos) is at root a road or path, but Scripture turns the image into a way of life — the whole direction and conduct of a person before God. To walk in a "way" is to live by a certain wisdom toward a certain end, and the Bible everywhere sets two roads before us: the way of the LORD and the way that perishes.

The Old Testament develops the picture morally and covenantally. The Psalter opens by contrasting them: "the LORD knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish" (Psalm 1:6). Israel is commanded to "walk in his ways" (Deuteronomy 8:6), and wisdom is a path that shines brighter until full day (Proverbs 4:18). God himself leads his people on the way through the wilderness, going before them in cloud and fire (Exodus 13:21). The prophets promise a guiding voice — "This is the way, walk in it" (Isaiah 30:21) — and a coming highway, "the Way of Holiness," on which the redeemed return to God (Isaiah 35:8). Above all, a herald is to "prepare the way of the LORD" through the desert (Isaiah 40:3).

The Gospels announce that this preparation has come: John the Baptist is the voice readying the LORD's way (Mark 1:2–3). Then Jesus astonishingly draws the whole image into himself, declaring it as a title: "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The path to God is no longer merely a code of conduct but a Person; to find the Father one must go by him.

So central was this that the earliest Christians were known simply as followers of "the Way." Saul sought authority against any "belonging to the Way" (Acts 9:2); Paul later confessed, "according to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our fathers" (Acts 24:14; cf. Acts 19:23). Hebrews completes the thought: through his torn flesh Jesus has opened "a new and living way" into God's very presence (Hebrews 10:20).

The motif therefore charts the gospel as pilgrimage. The God who led Israel through the wilderness has himself become the road; to walk the way of the LORD is now to follow Christ, in whom the journey and its destination — communion with the Father — are one and the same.
